original_abstract:
Recent progress in iridium-catalyzed borylation has provided powerful methods for functionalizing primary and secondary C–H bonds; however, the borylation of tertiary C–H bonds has remained a significant synthetic challenge. In this work, we report a broadly applicable method for the iridium-catalyzed undirected borylation of the tertiary bridgehead C–H bonds in bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs), bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes (BCHs), and hetero-bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes (XBCHs). 

This approach demonstrates high selectivity for the bridgehead position, even when in the presence of unactivated primary and secondary C–H bonds, and exhibits a wide functional group tolerance (>35 examples). The utility of the method is further illustrated by the late-stage modification of medicinal compounds and the gram-scale synthesis of versatile bicyclic building blocks. The resulting boronic esters serve as stable handles for the construction of carbon–carbon and carbon–heteroatom bonds, enabling the rapid diversification of critical bioisosteres. 

Intermolecular competition studies reveal that these bridgehead C–H bonds possess enhanced reactivity similar to that of cyclopropanes, which we attribute to the significant s-character of the strained C–H bond. Kinetic analysis and density functional theory (DFT) calculations suggest that C–H bond cleavage is reversible and that the turnover-limiting step is a stereoisomerization of the seven-coordinate iridium(V) intermediate that occurs prior to reductive elimination. This study provides a general strategy for the direct functionalization of hindered tertiary alkanes and facilitates the development of complex, three-dimensional hydrocarbon scaffolds for drug discovery.
humanized_abstract:
Iridium-catalyzed borylation has recently been shown to be an excellent method for functionalizing primary and secondary C–H bonds. Unfortunately, borylation of the most challenging tertiary C–H bonds remains elusive. Here we describe an iridium-catalyzed undirected borylation of the bridgehead tertiary C–H bonds in bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs), bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes (BCHs), and hetero-bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes (XBCHs).

This process shows a high level of regioselectivity for the bridgehead position, even in the presence of unactivated primary and secondary C–H bonds, and is compatible with more than 35 different functional groups. The applicability of this method has been demonstrated by late-stage functionalization of medicinally relevant molecules, as well as gram-scale synthesis of bicyclic cores, which serve as versatile building blocks. The boronic ester products can then be used as stable intermediates for subsequent carbon–carbon and carbon–heteroatom bond forming reactions, allowing for rapid access to diverse bioisosterics.

Bridgehead C–H bonds are functionalized using Ir(V). Intermolecular competition studies reveal that these bonds react faster than normal C–H bonds. Similar reactivity to that of cyclopropanes is proposed based on large s-character in strained C–H bonds. Kinetic analysis and density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal a reversible C–H bond activation with the turnover limiting stereoisomerization of the 7-coordinate iridium(V) species prior to reductive elimination. This work represents a general approach to the direct functionalization of tertiary, hindered alkanes and opens up routes to complex, three-dimensional, drug lead molecules.

document.output:

Recent progress in iridium-catalyzed borylation has provided powerful methods for functionalizing primary and secondary C–H bonds; however, the borylation of tertiary C–H bonds has remained a significant synthetic challenge.
